topics.headline: California May Revise — what the 2026-27 budget signals for charter intervention funding
topics.category: A (federal/state K-12 funding) — **lead topic from this lens**
topics.why_matters: Every Tier A partner runs on a Prop 98 / LCFF base plus categorical layers (Title I, Title III, ELO-P, Learning Recovery Emergency Block Grant residuals). The May Revise is the single document that tells charter business managers and EDs whether their intervention budget for next year holds, grows, or shrinks. A+ sits in exactly the spend category that gets cut first when categoricals tighten and protected hardest when CDE flags intervention as priority. Danielle's pitch ROI changes depending on which categorical column the money lives in.
topics.angle: Translate the May Revise into one sentence per category that matters to charter intervention budgets — LCFF, Title I carryover, Title III, ELO-P, Learning Recovery. No politics, just "here's what changed, here's what to ask your business manager Monday."
topics.roman_take: The conviction angle — *funded intervention works; underfunded intervention is theater.* Use the May Revise to argue that the schools who spend their categorical dollars on evidence-based, high-dosage tutoring outperform the ones who spread it thin across compliance line items.
topics.danielle_take: The implementation angle — a tactical checklist of "if your May Revise allocation moved X direction, here are the three categorical lines you should re-examine before June board." Practical, dated, school-business-manager language.

topics.headline: The post-ESSER intervention vendor collapse — and what districts losing their tutoring partner overnight should do next
topics.category: D (Industry/competitor moves), with strong overlap to B (K-12 problem stories) and A (funding)
topics.why_matters: Every venture-backed tutoring vendor that scaled on ESSER dollars is now contracting, pulling contracts, or pivoting to B2C. A+ never took that money, never built a burn-rate-dependent model, and is funded through stable Title I / Title III / LCFF supplemental dollars routed through charter partners. This is the cleanest differentiation moment A+ has had since the company was founded — "the vendors built for the sugar high are leaving the room; the vendors built for the work are still here." It also gives Danielle a direct outreach hook to any school whose previous tutoring vendor just walked.
topics.angle: Frame the shakeout factually (here's what's happening across the intervention vendor market), then position A+'s funding model and partner-embedded approach as the structural reason A+ is still standing. End with a soft call: if your previous tutoring partner just pulled out, here's what a Title-funded intervention partnership looks like.
topics.roman_take: "We watched everyone chase ESSER. We chose Title I and Title III. Here's why that choice looks different in May 2026 than it did in May 2022." Conviction angle: the funding source you build on determines whether you're still operating when the music stops.
topics.danielle_take: "When your previous tutoring vendor leaves, the first thing your kids lose isn't the sessions — it's the data continuity. Here's the implementation question every special programs coordinator should be asking the next vendor in the door." Implementation angle: vendor transition isn't a procurement problem, it's a student-progress problem.
